summary: High-energy set with smoking Truckin', Lazy>Supplication & tight jams
blurb: The first set builds momentum into a fiery Lazy Lightning → Supplication, while the second set delivers a soaring Truckin' followed by seamless transitions through Drums, Space and The Other One. Jerry’s vocals shine and Brent’s organ on The Other One adds extra color. Overall a high‑energy performance with only a brief guitar hiccup in the encore.
review: On August 31, 1980 at the Capital Centre, the Dead turned in a high‑octane show that still feels fresh for anyone hunting a late‑‘80s gem. The first set rolls from an upbeat Alabama Getaway into a standout Lazy Lightning → Supplication pairing, highlighted by Jerry’s sweet vocal delivery and Brent’s tasteful keyboards on Far From Me. The second set is the real fireworks: a smoking Truckin' that launches into a tight Drums‑Space excursion before morphing into a flawless The Other One → Stella Blue → I Need A Miracle → Good Lovin' sequence. Phil’s “dropping the bombs” bass and the double‑time shuffle from Mickey and Bill keep the momentum relentless, while Brent’s organ on The Other One adds an extra layer of sparkle. Even the encore – despite a brief loss of Jerry’s lead guitar – closes with a heartfelt Brokedown Palace that showcases his soft ballad side. Across multiple audience recordings the performance is consistently praised for its energy, tight segues and standout jams, making this one of the year’s top shows.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Six of eight recordings are explicitly positive, praising the high‑energy second set, standout Truckin', Lazy>Supplication and Brent’s organ on The Other One. One mixed review still calls the performance "phenomenal" while the lone negative is limited to technical pitch issues unrelated to playing. No consistent criticism of musical execution appears, so a strong positive sentiment (+1.4) is applied.

best_recording.reason: Very good audience tape praised as "gorgeous" and "slightly above average", capturing the full show with clear vocals and instruments; reviewers consider it the best source for this concert.

key_highlights: Smoking Truckin' into a seamless Drums‑Space transition
Lazy Lightning → Supplication pairing that glows throughout the night
Brent’s organ on The Other One and flawless segues give the set an almost rehearsed feel
Comes A Time shines as a centerpiece of the second set

band_performance.Jerry: Sweet, upfront vocals on most numbers; aggressive, bright guitar soloing especially on Truckin' and the second‑set jams, with a brief lead‑guitar loss in the encore but strong ballad playing on Brokedown Palace.
band_performance.Phil: Beautiful bass harmonics and rich low end; energetic "dropping the bombs" throughout, driving the jams forward even when other parts loosened up.
band_performance.Bob: Solid rhythm work backing Jerry’s solos; quirky vocal moment when Garcia’s gear was pulled; leads into Miracle and Good Lovin' with confident accompaniment.
band_performance.Brent: Pleasant keyboard pocket on Far From Me, solid organ tone on The Other One that stands out, and overall good mesh with the band’s dynamics.
band_performance.Mickey: Trippy drumming with hints of "Hamza al‑Din" flavor; very effective throughout the set, especially in the high‑energy second half.
band_performance.Billy: Double‑time shuffle adds drive to the groove; noted for strong percussion work though the Truckin' → drums transition is a bit rough.
